label: Assault
scenario_patterns: A person raises a fist and threatens to punch another in a coffeeshop dispute
A loan shark's runner makes threatening gestures at a debtor's door
A road rage driver exits the vehicle brandishing a weapon at another motorist
A person points an object resembling a weapon at another in a shopping mall
legal_tests: The defendant's act caused the plaintiff to reasonably apprehend the imminent application of force
The apprehension must be of immediate or imminent contact, not a future or conditional threat
The defendant must have intended to cause such apprehension (or been reckless as to causing it)
Words alone may constitute assault if accompanied by threatening conduct or circumstances
The plaintiff must actually apprehend the contact — a subjective requirement tempered by objective reasonableness
common_fact_patterns: Threats made during neighbourhood disputes in HDB estates
Road rage incidents where drivers threaten physical violence
Loan shark harassment involving intimidation at the victim's residence
Workplace bullying involving threats of physical harm
Brandishing weapons or weapon-like objects in public places
sg_case_references: Malcomson Nicholas Hugh Bertram v Mehta Naresh Kumar [2001] SGHC 308 — assault as a tort in the Singapore context
Wee Sia Tian v Long Thik Boon [1996] SGHC 84 — assault and battery arising from a physical altercation
Lee Tat Development Pte Ltd v Management Corporation Strata Title Plan No 301 [2009] SGCA 16 — discussed trespass to person including assault
Public Prosecutor v Lim Yee Hua [2018] SGHC 83 — criminal assault with discussion of elements applicable to the tort
party_roles: employer vicariously liable for employee's assault
defences: No reasonable apprehension of imminent contact was created
Consent — the plaintiff consented to the risk of such apprehension (e.g., mutual confrontation)
Self-defence — the defendant acted to prevent an imminent attack
Lawful authority — the defendant was exercising a lawful power (e.g., police officer)
The threat was conditional or related to the future, not imminent
